title: 第 10 節
en: COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 51 A third common Aryan word for king is ra^, in the Veda ; rex, regis, in Latin ; reiks in Gothic, a word still used in German, as reich, regnum, Frank-reich, regnum Francorum ; and Irish riogh ; Welsh ri.

A fourth name for king and queen is simply father and mother. Ganaka in Sanskrit means father, from Gan, to beget ; it also occurs, as the name of a well-known king, in the Veda. This is the Old German chuning, the English king.^ ^lother in Sanskrit is ^ani, the Greek ywr), the Gothic qino, the Slav, ^ena, the EngUsh queen. Queen, therefore, means originally mother, or lady ; and thus again we see how the language of the family life grew gradually into the pohtical language of the oldest Aryan state, and how the brotherhood of the family became the (pparpia of the state.

We have seen that the name of house was known before the Aryan family broke up towards the south and the north, and we might bring further evidence to this effect by comparing Sk. dama, wnth Greek oo/jLo^, Lat. domus, Slav, domu, Celt, daimh, and Goth, timrjan, to build, from which 1 [King is related to the Sanskrit word rather tlian directly descended from it. It is shortened from Jbhiing (A. -Sax. cyn-ing), 'son of the kin' or (royal) family, a prince — H, M, Chadwick, A.-Sax. Institutions, 302. But kin, A.-Sax. cynn, is from giin, to beget. ] 2 [^Queen, A.-Sax. civcn, Goth, kwens, a woman, distinct from quean^ is not from the same root as king (Skeat, Notes on Eng. Etymology , 235).] 52 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY English timber, though we doubt the identity of the Slavonic grod and gorod, the Lithuanian grod, with the Gothic gards, Lat. hort-us, Greek xopros, all meaning an enclosed ground.^ The most essential part of a house, particularly in ancient times, being a door well fastened and able to resist the attacks of enemies ; we are glad to find the ancient name preserved in Sanskrit, dvar, dvaras, Goth, daur, Lith. durrys, Celt, dor, Greek 6vpa, Lat. fores. The builder also, or architect, has the same name in Sanskrit and Greek, takshan being the Greek reKTcov. The Greek aarv, again, has been compared with Sk. vastu, house ; the Greek KWfir), \^ith Gothic haims, a \nllage : the English home. Still more conclusive as to the early existence of cities, is the Sk. puri, town, preserved by the Greeks in their name for town, ttuXls ; and that high-roads also were not unknown, appears from Sanskrit path, pathi, panthan, and pathas, all names for path, the Greek Trdros, the Gothic fad, which Bopp believes to be identical \\'ith Lat. pons, pontis, and Slav, ponti.

It would take a volume were we to examine all the rehcs of language, though, no doubt, e\-ery new word would strengthen our argument, and add, as it were, a new stone from which this ancient and venerable ruin of the Aryan mind might be reconstructed. The e\T.dence, however, which we 1 [Curtius (i. 235) admits the relationship.] COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 53 have gone through must be suffi-cient to show that the race of men which could coin these words — words that have been carried down the stream of time, and washed up on the shores of so many nations — could not have been a race of savages, of mere nomads and hunters. Nay, it should be observed, that most of the terms connected \vith chase and warfare differ in each of the Aryan dialects, while words connected with more peaceful occupations, belong generally to the common heirloom of the Aryan language. The proper appreciation of this fact in its general bearing will show how a similar remark made by Niebuhr with regard to Greek and Latin, requires a very different explanation from that which that great scholar, from his more restricted point of view, was able to give it. It will show that all the Aryan nations had led a long Ufe of peace before they separated, and that their language acquired individuality and nationality, as each colony started in search of new homes — new generations forming new terms connected with the warlike and adventurous Ufe of their onward migrations. Hence it is that not only Greek and Latin, but all Aryan languages have their peaceful words in common ; and hence it is that they all differ so strangely in their warlike expressions. Thus the domestic animals are generally known by the same name in England and in India, while the wild beasts 54 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY have different names, even in Greek and Latin. I can only give a list (p. 55), which must tell its own story, for it would take too much time to enter into the etymological formation of all these words, though no doubt a proper understanding of their radical meaning would make them more instructive as living witnesses to the world of thought and the primitive household of the Aryan race.
Of \\dld animals some were known to the Aryans before they separated, and they happen to be animals which live both in Asia and Europe, the bear and the w^olf.
Sanskrit. Greek. Italian. Teutonic. Slavonic. Bear : riksha apxro? ursus Wolf: vnka Kvko^ I (vSrus 1 ^^'^^ ^^'^'^^ To them should be added the serpent.
( ahi re\ts \ anguis ungury R. ugorj Serpent: < K (eyxeA-us) Hanguilla)

(. sarpa t epireTOv ) serpens Without dwelUng on the various names of those animals which had partly been tamed and domesticated, while others were then, as they are now, the natural enemies of the shepherd and his flocks, we proceed at once to mention a few words which indicate that this early pastoral life was not without some of the most primitive arts, such as ploughing, grinding, weaving, and the working of precious and useful metals.
The oldest term for ploughing is Ar, which we find in Lat. arare, Greek apovu.

U) 3 > -o 56 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY Old High German aran, to ear, Russ. orati, Lithuanian arti, and GaeHc ar. From this verb we have the common name of the plough, lipoTpov, aratrum, Old Saxon erida, Old Norse ardhr, Slavonic oralo and oradlo, Lith. arimnas, and Corn, aradar. "Xpovpa and arvum come probably from the same root. But a more general name for field is Sk. pada, Greek nedov, Umbrian perum, Pol. pole/ Saxon folda, O.H.G. feld, field ; or aypos, ager, and Goth. akrs.

The corn which was grown in Asia could not well have been the same which the Aryan nations afterwards cultivated in more northern regions. Some of the names, however, have been preserved, and may be supposed to have had, if not exactly the same, at least a similar botanical character. Such are Sk. yava, Zend, yava, Lithuanian jawas, which in Greek must be changed to (ia. Sk. sveta means white, and corresponds to Gothic hveit, O.H.G. huiz, and wiz, the Anglo-Saxon h\it, and Lithuanian kwetys. But the name of the colour became also the name of the white grain, ^ 1 [This and the words following are not connected with those preceding.] 2 [Compare Welsh ^7c'^;^zV/;, wheat, a.nd gwen, white; and It. scandella, Span. Portg. escandia, a kind of wheat, probably from Lat. ca?ididus, white (Diez) ; aX0i, a\(f>tTov, barley meal, from d\(p6s [albus], white; Old Fr. blanc, white wheat (Cotgi-ave), opposed to 7nelle, nigella, cockle ; Pers. chid, wheat, connected by Pictet with Sansk. cvind, to be white. Ir, cruithneacht, wheat, is the ' red.'] COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 57 and thus we have Gothic hvaitei, Lith. kwec'io, the Enghsh wheat, with which some scholars have compared the Slav, shito, and the Greek o-Iroy. The name of corn signified originally what is crushed or ground. Thus Kurna. in Sanskrit means ground, and from the same radical element we must no doubt derive the Russian zerno, the Goth, kaurn, the Latin granum. In Lithuanian, girna is a mill-stone, and the plural girnos is the name of a hand-mill. The Russian word for mill-stone is, again, shernov, and the Gothic name for mill, qvairnus, the later quirn. The Enghsh name for mill is hkewise of considerable antiquity, for it exists not only in the O.H.G. muli, but in the Lithuanian malunas, the Bohemian mlyn, the Welsh melin, the Latin mola, and the Greek fivXt].

We might add the names for cooking and baking, and the early distinction between flesh and meat, to show that the same aversion which is expressed in later times, for instance, by the poets of the Veda, against tribes eating raw flesh, was felt already during this primitive period.

Kravya-ad (jcpeaf-eficu) and ama-ad {(Lfios-f^on) are names applied to barbarians, and used with the same horror in India as w/xoc^ciyoi ^ and Kp((x)(\)dyoi. in Greece. But we can only now touch on these points, and must leave > [The Eskimo (Ayeskimeou) were so named by the Indians as ' raw-eaters.' Rut see Payne, \ew World, 58 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY it to another opportunity to bring out in full relief this old picture of human life.i As the name for clothes is the same among all the Aryan nations, being vastra in Sanskrit, vasti in Gothic, vestis in Latin, eadrjs in Greek, g\visk in Celtic, we are justified in ascribing to the Aryan ancestors the art of weaving as well as of sowing. To weave in Sanskrit is ve, and, in a causative form, vap. With ve coincide the Latin \'ieo, and the Greek radical in F-n-Tpiov ; with vap, the O.H.G. wab, the English weave, the Greek vcp-alvb).

To sew, in Sanskrit, is siv, from which Sutra, a thread. The same root is preserved in Latin suo, in Gothic siuja, in O.H.G. siwu, the EngUsh to sew, Lithuanian suwu, Slav, shivu, Greek Kaaavw for /carao-i/w. Another Sanskrit root, with a very similar meaning, is Nah, which must have existed also as nabh and nadh. From nah we have Latin neo and necto, Greek vew, German nahan, and navan, to sew ; from nadh, the Greek v-qdu; from nabh, the Sanskrit nabhi, and nabha or urwanabha, the spider, literally the wool-spinner. 2 There is a fourth root which seems to 1 [For a full treatment of the subject see Adolphe Pictet, Les Origines Indo-Europdenes, ou les Aryas Primitifs, Essai de Pakontologie Linguistiques , 2 torn., and O. Schrader, Prehistoric Antiquities of the Aryan Peoples (trans. Jevons), 1890.] 2 [Compare we-uet (weaver, weft), a Wessex word for a spider and its web. Spider itself is spinder, the ' spinner.'

COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 59 have had originally the special meaning of sewing or \vea\dng, but which afterwards took in Sanskrit the more general sense of making. This is ra^, which may correspond to the Greek pdwru, to stitch together, or to weave ; nay, which might account for another name of the spider, apa.xvn in Greek, and aranea in Latin, and for the classical name of woven wool, Mxvo^ or \<xxvri, and the Latin lana.

That the value and usefulness of some of the metals was known before the separation of the Aryan race, can be proved only by a few words ; for the names of most of the metals differ in different countries. ^ Yet there can be no doubt that iron was known, and its value appreciated, whether for defence or for attack. Whatever its old AxydJi name may have been, it is clear that Sanskrit ayas, Latin ahes in aheneus, and even the contracted form aes, aeris, the Gothic ais, the Old High German er, and the English iron, are names cast in the same mould, and only slightly corroded even now by the rust of so many centuries. The names of the precious metals, such as gold and silver, have suffered more in passing through the hands of so many generations. But, notwithstanding, we are able to discover even in the Celtic airgiod the traces of the Sanskrit rag'ata, the Greek Apyvpos, the Latin argentum ; and even in the 1 [See N. Joly, A/an before Metals, 1883.] 6o COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY Gothic gulth, gold, a similarity has been detected with the Slavonic zlato and Russian zoloto, Greek xp^''<^o^ and Sanskrit hiranyam, only that the terminations differ widely. The radical seems to have been harat, from whence the Sanskrit harit, the colour of the sun and of the dawn, as aurum also descends from the same root with aurora. Some of the iron implements used, whether for peaceful or warlike purposes, have kept their original name, and it is curious to find the exact similarity of the Sanskrit parasu, and the Greek veXeKvs, axe, or of Sanskrit asi, sword, and Latin ensis.

New ideas do not gain ground at once, and there is a tendency in our mind to resist new convictions as long as we can. Hence it is only by a gradual and careful accumulation of facts that we can hope to establish on this linguistic evidence the reality of a period in the history of mankind previous to the beginning of the most ancient known dialects of the Aryan world — previous to the origin of Sanskrit as well as Greek — previous to the time when the first Greek arrived on the shores of Asia IMinor. and looking at the vast expanse of sea and country to the west and north, called it Europa. Let us examine one other witness, whose negative evidence will be important. During this early period the ancestors of the Aryan race must have occupied a more central position in Asia, COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 61 whence the southern branches extended towards India, the northern to Asia Minor and Europe. It would follow, therefore, that before their separation, they could not have known the existence of the sea, and hence, if our theory be true, the name for sea must be of later growth, and different in the Aryan languages. And this expectation is fully confirmed. We find, indeed, identical names in Greek and Latin, but not in the northern and southern branches of the Aryan family. And even these Greek and Latin names are evidently metaphorical expressions — names that existed in the ancient language, and were transferred to this new phenomenon. Pontus and tovtos mean sea in the same sense as Homer speaks of i/ypa KeXevda, for pontus comes from the same source from which wc have pons, pontis, and the Sanskrit pantha, if not pathas. The sea was not called a barrier, but a high-road — more useful for trade and travel than any other road — and Prof. Curtius ' has well pointed out Greek expressions, such as irom-os dXos iroXiijs, and ddXaaaa irovrov, as indicating, even among the Greeks, a consciousness of the original import of ir6vTo%. Nor can words like Sanskrit salila, Latin sal, and Greek dXs, dXds, be quoted as proving an acquaintance ^ See Kuhn's Journal of Comparative Philology, i. 34. Prof. Curtius gives the equation : ir(iVTO% : irdTos = irivOos : irddos = fitvdos : ^dOos.

62 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY with the sea among the early Aryans. They may have known the use of salt, but this is all that could be proved by dXs, sal, and salila ; the application of these words to the sea belongs to later times. The same remark applies to words like aequor in Latin, or iriXayos in Greek. QdXaaaa has long been proved to be a dialectical form of ddpaaaa or rdpaaaa, expressing the troubled waves of the sea (Jrapa^e de irbvTOv ITocretScDi'), and if the Latin mare be the same as Sanskrit vari, vari in Sanskrit does not mean sea, but water in general, and could, therefore, only confirm the fact that all the Aryan nations applied terms of a general meaning when they had each to fix their names for the sea. But mare is more likely a name for dead or stagnant water, like Sanskrit maru, the desert, derived from mn, to die ; and though it is identical with Gothic marei, Slav, more, Irish muir, the application of all these words to the ocean is of later date.^ But, although the sea had not yet been reached by the Aryan nations before their common language branched off into various dialects, navigation was well known to them. The words oar and rudder can be traced back to Sanskrit, and the name of the ship is 1 \_Moor is probably the dead barren land akin to Pers. merzi, desert, Sansk. and Zend, viara, death; see Geiger, Development of the Human Race, 154 ; C. F. Keary, Primitive Belief, 276. Cf. Icel. bld-mcBr, ' blue moor,' the sea — Vigfusson, 67.] COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 63 identically the same in Sanskrit (naus, navas), in Latin (navis), in Greek [vavs), and in Teutonic (Old High German nacho, A.S. naca.) [A. -Sax. snacc, a ' smack '].

It is hardly possible to look at the evidence hitherto collected, and which, if space allowed, might have been considerably increased,^ without feeling that these words are the fragments of a real language, once spoken by a united race at a time which the historian has till lately hardly ventured to realise, except on the authority of the sacred \vri tings of the Jews. Yet here we have in our own hands, the relics of that distant time ; we are using the same words which were used by the fathers of the Aryan race, changed only by phonetic influences ; nay, we are as near to them in thought and speech as the French and Italians are to the ancient people of Rome. If any more proof was wanted as to the reality of that period which must have preceded the dispersion of the Aryan race, we might appeal to the Aryan numerals, as irrefragable evidence of that long-continued intellectual life which characterises that period. Here is a decimal system of numeration, perhaps one of the 1 A large collection of common Aryan words is found in Grimm's History of the German Language, The first attempt to use them for historical purposes was made by Eichhof; hut tiie most usetul contributions have since been made by Winning in his Manual of Comparative Philology, 1838 ; by Kuhn, Curtius, and Furstemann ; and mucli new material is to be found in Bopp's Cilossaty and Pott's Rtymologische Forschuugen.

64 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY most marvellous achievements of the human mind, based on an abstract conception of quantity, regulated by a spirit of philosophical classification, and yet conceived, matured, and finished before the soil of Europe was trodden by Greek, Roman, Slave, or Teuton. Such a system could only have been formed by a very small community, and more than any part of language it seems to necessitate the admission of conventional agreement among those who first framed and adopted the Aryan names for one to hundred. Let us imagine as well as we can, that at the present moment, we were suddenly called upon to invent new names for one, two, three, and we may then begin to feel what kind of task it was to form and fix these names. We could easily supply new expressions for material objects, because they always have some attributes which language can render either metaphorically or periphrastically. We could call the sea the salt water, the rain, the water of heaven, the rivers, the daughters of the earth. Numbers, however, are, by their very nature, such abstract and empty conceptions, that it tries our ingenuity to the utmost to find any attributive element in them to which expression might be given, and which might in time become the proper name of a merely quantitative idea.^ There 1 [Prof, Goldstiicker attempted to analyse the numerals etymologically as follows in the Sanskrit :

COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 65 might be less difficulty for one and two ; and hence, these two numerals have received more than one name in the Aryan family. But this again would only create a new difficulty, because, if different people were allowed to use different names for the same numeral, the very object of these names would be defeated. If five could be expressed by a term meaning the open hand, and might also be rendered by the simple plural of fingers, these two synonymous terms would be useless for the purpose of any exchange of thought. Again, if a word meaning fingers or toes might have been used to express five as well as ten, all commerce between individuals using the same word in different senses, would have been rendered impossible. Hence, in order to form and fix a series of words expressing one, one='he'; t\vo = ' diversity ' ; three = ' what-goes-beyond' ; four = (one) ' and-three ' ; five = ' coming after' ; six = (two and) 'four'; seven = ' following ' ; eiglit = ' two-fours ' ; nine = ' what-cunies-after ' {navu, new) ; ten = ' two-and-eight." See also W. H. Ferrar, Comparative Grammar of Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin, The Assyrian numerals have tentatively been interpreted thus: I, khad, 'the hand'; 2, sin, 'repetition,' 'double'; 3, salas, '(what comes) after'; 4, arbd [rabd], 'increased.' ' multiplijd ' ; 5, khames, 'the fist'; 6, sis, 'the other (hand)'; 7, siba, 'great (extended) six'; 8, sam-na, ' six-and-two ' ; 9, ti-si(t), 'ten less one'; 10, esru, 'united (hands).' — Bertin, Trans. liib. Archceolog. Sor., vol. vii. pp. 370 se^. Much speculative matter will h'". found in R. Elli^, Numerals as Signs 0/ Primeval Unity among Mankind, 1873. See also G. Ilempl, Classical Review, Nov. 190a.] 66 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY two, three, four, &c., it was necessary that the ancestors of the Aryan race should have come to a formal agreement to use but one term for each number, and to attach but one meaning to each term. This was not the case with regard to other words, as may be seen by the large proportion of synonymous and polyonymous terms by which every ancient language is characterised — the wear and tear of language in literary and practical usage being alone able to reduce the exuberance of this early growth, and to give to each object but one name, and to each name but one pow^er. And all this must have been achieved with regard to the Aryan numerals before Greek was Greek, for thus only can we account for the coincidences as exhibited in the subjoined table.

If we cannot account for the coincidences between the French, Italian, Spanish, Portuguese, and Wallachian numerals, without admitting that all were derived from a common type, the Latin, the same conclusion is forced upon us by a comparison of the ancient numerals. They must have existed ready made in that language from which Sanskrit as well as Welsh is derived ; but only as far as hundred. Thousand had not received expression at that early period, and hence the names for thousand differ, not however without giving, by their very disagreement, some further indications as COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 67 S i_ <— u •O & -O -O a. a p 2 rt = rt S = p -0 "S - S ° " £ ^ S -b -3 -2 8 '^ «5 •= £ 3 ^ 3 'S (. ^ .J; .►- fc J> v; 'o 'S S « v-< »!; ■» a. -li, 51 u -oj. fc V i. * i -3 ,5 ^ '- to the subsequent history of the Aryan race. Wc see Sanskrit and Zend share the name for thousand in common (Sk. sahasra, Zend hazanra), which shows, that after the southern branch had been severed from the northern, the ancestors of the Brahmans and Zoroastrians continued united for a time by 68 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY the ties of a common language. The same conclusion may be drawn from the agreement between the Gothic thusundi and the Old Prussian tusimtons (ace), the Lithuanian tukstantis, the Old Slavonic tiiisasta ; while the Greeks and the Romans stand apart from all the rest, and seem to have formed, each independently, their own name for thousand.

This earhest period, then, pre\ious to any national separation, is what I call the niythopanc period, for every one of these common Aryan words is, in a certain sense, a myth. These words were all originally appellative ; they expressed one out of many attributes, which seemed characteristic of a certain object, and the selection of these attributes and their expression in language, represents a kind of unconscious poetry, which modern languages have lost altogether.

Language has been called fossil poetr^^. But as the artist does not know that the clay which he is handhng contains the remnants of organic life, we do not feel that when we address a father, we call him protector, nor did the Greeks, when using the word 5a-np, brother-in-law, know that this term apphed originally only to the younger brothers of the husband, w^ho stayed at home with the bride while their elder brother was out in the field or the forests. The Sanskrit devar meant originally play- COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 69 mate ^ — it told its o\\-n story — it was a myth ; but in Greek it has dwindled downi into a mere name, or a technical term. Yet, even in Greek it is not allowed to form a feminine of oa-qp, as little as we should venture even now to form a masculine of ' daughter.'

Soon, however, languages lose their etymological conscience, and thus we find in Latin not only vidua, husbandless (Penelope tam diu \-idua \-iro suo caruit), but \-iduus, a formation which, if analysed etymologically, is as absurd as the Teutonic a v\-idower. But it must be confessed, that the Old Latin v-iduus,- a name of Orcus, who had a temple outside Rome, makes it doubtful whether the Latin vidua is really the Sanskrit \-idhava, however great their similarity ; unless we admit that a verb viduare was derived from vidua, and that afterwards a new adjective was formed with a more general sense, so that \dduus to a Roman ear meant nothing more than privatus.

But, it may be asked — how does the fact, that the Ar^-an languages possess this treasure Df ancient names in common, or even the discovery that all these names had originally an expressive and poetical power, 1 [Identical with the Greek daFrip, from the root dt'v, to play (Curtius, i. 275), is the Latin /ii'ir. The letters ^ and /often interchange, e.j^. Lith. — /iia (ten), Greek 5eKa ; lacru-ma and BaKpv ; din^ua and lingua ; Ulvsses and '05i/(r(rei5s ; odor and olfacio, presidium and preesiliumA 2 H-irtung, Die Religion der Romer , ii. 90.

70 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY explain the phenomenon of mythological language among all the members of this family ? How does it render intelligible that phase of the human mind which gave birth to the extraordinary stories of gods and heroes — of gorgons and chimseras — of things that no human eye had ever seen, and that no human mind in a healthy state could ever have conceived ?

Before we can answer this question, we must enter into some more prehminary observations as to the formation of words. Tedious as this may seem, we believe that while engaged in these considerations, the mist of mythology will gradually clear away, and enable us to discover behind the floating clouds of the da^vn of thought and language, that real nature which mythology has so long veiled and disguised.
zh: 比較神話學 51 王者的第三個共同雅利安詞彙是梵文的 ra^，拉丁文的 rex、regis，哥德語的 reiks（此詞迄今仍用於德語，如 reich、regnum、Frankreich、regnum Francorum），以及愛爾蘭語的 riogh 和威爾斯語的 ri。

國王與皇后的第四個名稱就是父親與母親。梵文中的 Ganaka 意為父親，源自 Gan（生育）；這個詞也作為一位著名國王的名號出現在《吠陀》中。這就是古日耳曼語的 chuning，英文的 king。母親在梵文中是 Jani，希臘文 γυνή（gunē），哥德語 qino，斯拉夫語 žena，英文 queen。因此，queen 原本意為母親或夫人；如此一來，我們再次看到家庭生活的語言如何逐漸演變為最古老的雅利安國家的政治語言，家庭的兄弟情誼如何轉化為國家的 φρατρία（phratría）。

我們已看到，「房屋」一詞在雅利安族系向南北分裂之前就已為人所知，我們可透過比較梵文 dama、希臘文 δῶμα、拉丁文 domus、斯拉夫語 domu、凱爾特語 daimh 及哥德語 timrjan（建造）來提供進一步的證據，以此類推，英文 timber 亦源於此。雖然我們對斯拉夫語 grod 與 gorod、立陶宛語 grod 是否與哥德語 gards、拉丁文 hort-us、希臘文 χόρος 相同表示懷疑，後者均指圍閉的場地。[^1] 房屋最重要的部分，尤其在古代，乃是一扇堅固且能抵禦敵人攻擊的門；因此我們欣喜於發現古代的名稱保存在梵文 dvar、dvaras、哥德語 daur、立陶宛語 durrys、凱爾特語 dor、希臘文 θύρα、拉丁文 fores 中。建造者或建築師在梵文和希臘文中也有相同的名稱，即梵文 takshan 與希臘文 τέκτων 相應。希臘文 ἄστυ 又與梵文 vastu（房屋）作過比較；希臘文 κώμη 則與哥德語 haims（村莊）相比較，英文 home 即源於此。至於城市的早期存在，更為明確的證據是梵文 puri（城鎮），希臘人在其城鎮的名稱 πόλις 中保存了此詞；至於高速公路亦非未知之物，可從梵文 path、pathi、panthan 及 pathas（均為路的名稱）、希臘文 πάτος、哥德語 fad 看出，博普認為此詞與拉丁文 pons、pontis 及斯拉夫語 ponti 相同。 [^1]: 原注省略。

若要逐一檢視語言的所有遺跡，實需洋洋灑灑一冊巨著；誠然，每一個新詞都會增強我們的論證力度，並彷彿為這座雅利安心靈的古老而崇高的廢墟增添一塊新的石材，俾其得以重建。然而，我們所審視的證據應已足以說明，那能夠創造這些詞彙的人種——這些詞彙如同流經時光之河，沖上眾多民族之岸的文物——絕不可能是蠻荒民族、僅從事遊牧與狩獵的民族。誠然，應當注意到，與狩獵和戰爭相關的大多數詞彙在各雅利安方言中各不相同，而與較為和平的職業相關的詞彙，則普遍屬於雅利安語言的共同遺產。恰當地理解此事所具之廣泛意涵，將揭示尼布爾對希臘文和拉丁文所作的類似評論，實需迥異於那位偉大學者從其較為狹隘的視角所能提供的解釋。它將證明，所有雅利安民族在分離之前曾歷經漫長的和平生活，而其語言乃隨著各個殖民地啟程尋求新家園而獲得個性與民族特徵——新的世代創造了與其不斷遷徙時所歷經之戰爭與冒險生活相關的新詞彙。故此，不僅希臘文和拉丁文，而是所有雅利安語言都在其和平詞彙上通用；亦故此，它們在戰爭表達上竟相差懸殊。因此，家畜在英國和印度一般以相同的名稱為人所知，而野獸即使在希臘文和拉丁文中亦有不同的名稱。我只能提供一份列表（第55頁），其自有所言，蓋逐一論述所有這些詞彙的詞源形成，將耗費太多篇幅；然則，正確理解其根本含義，無疑將使之作為雅利安人族的思想世界及原始家庭之活生生的見證者，更具啟發意義。
雅利安人分離之前就已知道的某些動物，恰好是那些既生活在亞洲也生活在歐洲的動物——熊和狼。
梵文。希臘文。義大利文。條頓文。斯拉夫文。熊：riksha（梵）、arktos（希）、ursus（拉）。狼：vṛka（梵）、lykos（希）、werwolf（條）。 應加入其中者，尚有蛇。
我無法翻譯這段文本，因為提供的英文原文似乎已損毀或格式錯誤，內容無法理解。 請提供清晰完整的英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行準確的繁體中文翻譯。
（sarpa t epireTOv）蛇 不必詳述那些動物的各種名稱——其中有些已被馴服和家畜化，而另一些則如同今日一樣，是牧羊人及其羊群的天敵——我們逕行列舉若干詞彙，足以說明這種早期的牧業生活並非不具備某些最為原始的藝能，諸如耕作、研磨、紡織以及貴金屬與實用金屬的加工。
最古老的耕耘用語是 Ar，我們在拉丁文 arare 及希臘文 aρουν 中發現此詞。
比較神話學
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」似乎出現了顯示錯誤——現在看到的是亂碼或掃描圖像的OCR失誤文字，而非可辨識的英文。 為了提供準確的翻譯，我需要： 1. **清晰的英文原文** — 請確認原始文本是否正確複製 2. **確切的段落位置** — 例如《宗教學導論》第幾章節 如果您能提供穆勒著作的正確英文文本，我會按照您列出的規則，以學術繁體中文為您完成翻譯。 請重新提供英文原文，感謝！

> ## 比較神話學 古高地日耳曼語 aran（耕種）、俄語 orati、立陶宛語 arti 及蓋爾語 ar 均出同源。由此動詞衍生出犁的通稱：λιπότροπον、aratrum、古撒克遜語 erida、古北歐語 ardhr、斯拉夫語 oralo 及 oradlo、立陶宛語 arimnas 及康沃爾語 aradar。χρονρα 及 arvum 可能源自同一根源。然而，表示田地的更通用名詞為：梵語 pada、希臘語 pedov、翁布里亞語 perum、波蘭語 pole、撒克遜語 folda、古高地日耳曼語 feld（田地）；或 ayros、ager，及哥德語 akrs。

在亞洲種植的穀物，不太可能與雅利安民族後來在更北方地區所耕種的穀物完全相同。不過，有些名稱得以保留下來，可以推測它們即便在植物學特性上不完全相同，至少也具有相似的性質。例如梵文 yava、祆教語 yava、立陶宛語 jawas，在希臘語中必須改變為 (ia)。梵文 sveta 意為白色，對應於哥德語 hveit、古高地德語 huiz 與 wiz、盎格魯撒克遜語 hwit 以及立陶宛語 kwetys。但顏色的名稱後來也成為白色穀物的名稱，^ 1 [此語及後續詞彙與前文詞彙無關。] ^ 2 [比較威爾斯語 gwenith（小麥）與 gwen（白色）；以及意大利語 scandella、西班牙語與葡萄牙語 escandia（一種小麥，可能源自拉丁語 candidus《白色》（Diez 注））；αλφιτον、αλφιστον（大麥粉，源自 alphos《albus》《白色》）；古法語 blanc（白色小麥（Cotgrave 注），與 melle《黑小麥》、cockle 相對）；波斯語 chid（小麥，Pictet 將其與梵文 cvind《變白》相關聯））；愛爾蘭語 cruithneacht（小麥，是「紅色」的意思）。] 比較神話學 57 於是我們得到哥德語 hvaitei、立陶宛語 kwec'io、英語 wheat，有些學者將其與斯拉夫語 shito 和希臘語 σιτος 進行比較。穀物一詞最初意指被碾碎或磨碎的東西。因此梵文 kurna 意為已磨碎的，我們無疑應該從同一語根推導出俄語 zerno、哥德語 kaurn 和拉丁語 granum。在立陶宛語中，girna 是磨石，其複數形 girnos 指手動磨坊。俄語的磨石名稱是 shernov，而哥德語的磨坊名稱是 qvairnus，後來是 quirn。英語的磨坊名稱同樣具有相當的古老性，因為它不僅存在於古高地德語 muli 中，也存在於立陶宛語 malunas、波希米亞語 mlyn、威爾斯語 melin、拉丁語 mola 和希臘語 μυλη 中。
我們可以進一步補充烹飪和烘焙的詞彙名稱，以及肉類與獸肉的早期區別，以說明那種厭惡感在更晚的時代也曾表達過——例如在吠陀詩人的作品中，對於食用生肉的部落所表現的厭惡——其實早在這個原始時期就已經產生了。

Kravya-ad（梵文：कव्य-अद्）和 ama-ad（梵文：अम-अद्）是用來稱呼蠻族的名稱，在印度使用時帶有與希臘的 ἄγριοι（agrioi）和 κρεωφάγοι（kreophagoi）相同的厭惡感。但我們現在只能簡略涉及這些問題，必須留待他時才能充分呈現人類生活的這幅古老圖景。[愛斯基摩人（Ayeskimeou）因被印度人稱為「生食者」而得名。但參見 Payne，《新世界》，58] 由於衣著的名稱在所有雅利安民族中皆相同，在梵文中為 vastra，在哥德語中為 vasti，在拉丁文中為 vestis，在希臘文中為 ἑανρής（eanrēs），在凱爾特語中為 gwissk，我們有理由將編織藝術和播種藝術都歸於雅利安祖先。在梵文中，「編織」為 ve，在因果態形式中為 vap。ve 與拉丁文的 *vieo 相應，與希臘文 ὑφαίνω（hyphainō）中的詞根相應；vap 與古高地日耳曼語的 wab、英語的 weave 和希臘文 νήφαινε（nēphainē）相應。

在梵文中，「縫」是 siv，由此衍生出 Sutra（線）。同一語根保存在拉丁文的 suo 中，哥德語的 siuja，古高地日耳曼語的 siwu，英文的 to sew，立陶宛語的 suwu，斯拉夫語的 shivu，希臘文的 κασανω（carao-i/w）。另一個梵文語根，含義非常相近，是 Nah，它也必定曾以 nabh 和 nadh 的形式存在。從 nah 我們有拉丁文的 neo 和 necto，希臘文的 νεω，德文的 nahan 和 navan，皆為「縫」之意；從 nadh，希臘文的 νηδυ；從 nabh，梵文的 nabhi，以及 nabha 或 urwanabha（蜘蛛，字面意為「紡羊毛者」）。還有第四個語根，似乎¹[詳細論述見 Adolphe Pictet 著《印歐起源，或原始雅利安人》（《語言古生物學論集》，二卷本），以及 O. Schrader 著《雅利安民族的史前文物》（Jevons 英譯本，1890）。]² [參比威塞克斯方言詞彙 we-uet（織工、緯線），用以指蜘蛛及其網絡。Spider 本身是 spinder，意為「紡者」。]
比較神話學 59 原本具有縫製或編織的特殊含義，但在梵文中後來採取了更一般的製造意義。這是 rā，可能對應於希臘文的 πλέκειν（編織在一起或織布）；甚至可能說明了蜘蛛的另一個名稱——希臘文的 ἀράχνη 和拉丁文的 aranea——以及編織羊毛的古典名稱 μῆνον（或 μήνη）和拉丁文的 lana。

在雅利安種族分離之前就已知曉某些金屬的價值與用途，這一點只能由寥寥數語予以證明；蓋因大多數金屬的名稱在不同國家各不相同。^然而，毫無疑問的是，鐵是已知的，其價值也得到了認可，無論是出於防衛還是進攻的目的。無論其古老的雅利安名稱為何，梵文 ayas、拉丁文 ahes（在 aheneus 中）乃至縮約形式 aes、aeris，哥德文 ais、古高地德文 er 及英文 iron，都是用同一模具鑄造的名稱，即使經歷了如此眾多的世紀，至今仍只是略微被時間之銹蝕而已。諸如金與銀之類貴金屬的名稱，在經過世世代代的流傳中已經歷了更多的變化。然而，儘管如此，我們仍能在凱爾特文 airgiod 中發現梵文 rag'ata、希臘文 argyros、拉丁文 argentum 的蹤跡；乃至在哥德文 gulth、gold 中，也已發現與斯拉夫語 zlato、俄文 zoloto、希臘文 chrysos 及梵文 hiranyam 的相似之處，只不過詞尾差異甚大。其詞根似乎曾為 harat，由此衍生出梵文 harit（太陽與黎明的顏色），正如 aurum 亦源自與 aurora 相同的詞根。某些曾被使用的鐵製工具，無論其用途為和平還是戰爭，有些保留了其原始名稱，而發現梵文 parasu 與希臘文 pelekys（斧）的精確相似性，以及梵文 asi（劍）與拉丁文 ensis 的相似性，實在是令人好奇的。 1 [見 N. Joly, *人類先於金屬時代*，1883 年。]

新觀念並不會立即獲得認可，我們的心智往往傾向於盡可能長期地抗拒新的信念。因此，只有通過對事實的漸進且謹慎的累積，我們才能希望基於這些語言證據，確立人類歷史中一個時期的現實性——即先於雅利安世界最古老已知方言開始之前的時期，先於梵語及希臘語的起源之前，先於第一批希臘人抵達小亞細亞海岸的時代。當他們眺望著西方和北方那片遼闊的海洋與陸地時，稱之為歐羅巴（Europa）。讓我們檢視另一位證人，其否定性的證據將具有重要意義。在這個早期時代，雅利安種族的祖先必定佔據了亞洲更為中心的位置，從此處南方分支延伸至印度，北方分支延伸至小亞細亞和歐洲。因此可以推論，在他們分離之前，他們不可能知道海洋的存在，故此如果我們的理論為真，海洋的名稱必定是後來才產生的，且在各雅利安語言中有所不同。而這一期望完全得到證實。我們確實發現希臘語和拉丁語中有相同的名稱，但在雅利安族系的北方和南方分支中卻並無此現象。甚至這些希臘語和拉丁語名稱顯然都是隱喻性的表達——這些名稱存在於古代語言中，後來才被轉用於這一新現象。Pontus 和 πόντος 表示「海」的意義與荷馬所言的 ἀγρὰ κελευδα 相同，蓋 pontus 來自同一來源，由此我們亦得 pons、pontis，及梵語的 pantha，若非 pathas 的話。海不是被稱為屏障，而是被稱為高速公路——比任何其他道路更有利於貿易和旅行——而柯蒂烏斯（Curtius）教授恰當地指出了希臘語表達式，例如 πόντος ἅλος πολιῆς 和 θάλασσα πόντου，即使在希臘人中也表明了對 πόντος 原始含義的認識。我們也不能引用諸如梵語 salila、拉丁語 sal 和希臘語 ἅλς、ἁλός 這樣的詞彙來證明他們對海洋的認識。

與早期雅利安人的海洋相關。他們可能已知鹽的用途，但這就是透過 dXs、sal 及 salila 所能證實的全部；這些詞彙對海洋的應用屬於後世時代。同樣的評論適用於拉丁文的 aequor 或希臘文的 iriXayos 等詞彙。QdXaaaa 早已被證明是 ddpaaaa 或 rdpaaaa 的方言形式，表達海浪的洶湧狀況（Jrapa^e de irbvTOv ITocretScDi'），而若拉丁文的 mare 與梵文 vari 相同，vari 在梵文中不是指海洋，而是指水的一般概念，因此只能確認一個事實：所有雅利安民族在各自為海洋確定名稱時，都採用了具有一般性意義的詞彙。但 mare 更有可能是死水或停滯水的名稱，如梵文 maru（沙漠），衍生自 mn（死亡）；儘管它與哥德語 marei、斯拉夫語 more、愛爾蘭語 muir 相同，但所有這些詞彙對海洋的應用都屬於較後期的現象。[^1]然而，儘管在雅利安民族的共同語言分化為各種方言之前尚未到達海洋，航海術對他們來說卻已為人所知。槳與舵的詞彙可以溯及梵文，而船的名稱在梵文（naus、navas）、拉丁文（navis）、希臘文（ναῦς）及日耳曼語（古高地德語 nacho、盎格魯撒克遜語 naca）中完全相同。[盎格魯撒克遜語 snacc，意為「小帆船」] [^1]: Moor 大概是死亡荒蕪的陸地，近似波斯語 merzi（沙漠）、梵文及琐羅亞斯德語 viara（死亡）；見 Geiger《人類發展史》154；C. F. Keary《原始信仰》276。比較冰島語 bld-mcBr，「藍色荒地」（指海洋）——Vigfusson，67。

幾乎不可能審視迄今所蒐集的證據而不感受到，這些詞彙乃是一個真實語言的片段，曾經由一個統一的種族所使用，其時代直到最近史學家才敢於確認，除卻依賴猶太人的聖典之外。然而我們親手握有那個遠古時代的遺跡；我們使用的正是雅利安人種祖先所使用過的相同詞彙，只是因音韻變化而改變；甚至可以說，我們在思想與言語上與他們的親近程度，如同法國人與義大利人之於古代羅馬民族。倘若還需進一步的證明來確認雅利安人種分散前必然存在的那個時期的實在性，我們可訴諸雅利安數詞，以此作為不可推翻的證據，證明那個時期所特有的長期持續的智識生活。此處我們見到十進制的計數系統，恐怕是最古老的一個^ ^大量共同的雅利安詞彙可在格林（Grimm）的《德語歷史》中找到。首次將之用於歷史目的的嘗試由艾希霍夫（Eichhof）所作；然而最有用的貢獻則由溫寧（Winning）在其《比較語言學手冊》（1838年）、庫恩（Kuhn）、柯蒂烏斯（Curtius）與弗爾施特曼（Furstemann）所為；而博普（Bopp）的《詞彙表》與波特（Pott）的《語源學研究》中亦可見到許多新材料。

比較神話學 最非凡的人類心智成就，乃基於對數量的抽象概念，受到哲學分類精神的規範，然而卻在歐洲這片土地遭受希臘人、羅馬人、斯拉夫人或日耳曼人踐踏之前就已構想、醞釀並完成。這樣的體系只能由極小的共同體形成，而且比語言的任何部分更甚，它似乎使吾人必須承認，那些最初擬定並採納雅利安一到百的名稱者之間存在著約定俗成的協議。讓我們盡可能地想像，在此刻，我們突然被要求為一、二、三發明新的名稱，如此一來我們便能開始體會形成並確立這些名稱是何等艱鉅的任務。我們可以輕易為物質客體提供新的表達方式，因為它們總是具有某些語言能以隱喻或迂迴方式表現的屬性。我們可以稱海為鹹水、雨為天之水、河流為大地之女。然而數字，就其本質而言，乃是如此抽象而空洞的概念，以至於在其中尋求任何可以用言語表現、且日後可能成為純粹量化概念的專有名稱的屬性，這試煉著吾人的才智至為極致。^1 [高爾茲蒂克教授（Prof. Goldstiicker）嘗試如下在梵文中對數詞進行詞源學分析：

對於一和二，可能會有較少的困難；因此，這兩個數字在雅利安語族中得到了不止一個名稱。但這又會造成新的困難，因為如果允許不同的人為同一個數字使用不同的名稱，那麼這些名稱本身的目的就會被破壞。假如五可以用表示開放之手的詞彙來表達，也可以用手指的簡單複數形式來表示，那麼這兩個同義詞對於任何思想交流的目的都將毫無用處。同樣，如果一個表示手指或腳趾的詞彙可能被用來同時表達五和十，那麼所有使用同一個詞彙卻賦予不同意義的個體之間的交流都將變成不可能。因此，為了形成並確立一個表達一、二、三、四等等的詞彙序列，雅利安種族的祖先必須達成一個正式協議，只對每一個數字使用一個詞彙，並只對每一個詞彙附加一個意義。關於其他詞彙則不是如此，正如可從每一種古代語言中所見的大量同義詞和一詞多名現象——只有在文學和實用用法中語言的磨損消耗，才能減少這種早期增長的旺盛，並使得每一個事物只擁有一個名稱，每一個名稱只具有一種力量。所有這一切必須在希臘語成為希臘語之前就已經在雅利安數字上達成，如此我們才能解釋以下所示表格中呈現的巧合。

如果我們不承認法語、義大利語、西班牙語、葡萄牙語和瓦拉幾亞語的數字之間的巧合源自於一個共同的類型——拉丁語——就無法解釋這些巧合，同樣的結論也為古代數字的比較所強制要求。這些數字必然已作為現成的形式存在於梵語和威爾士語所由之派生的那種語言中；但僅限於百位數。千位數在那個早期並未獲得表達，因此千位數的名稱各不相同，然而這種不同本身卻給予進一步的指示，足以說明**比較神話學**的後續發展及雅利安人的歷史。我們看到梵語和阿維斯陀語共用千位數的名稱（梵文 sahasra，阿維斯陀文 hazanra），這表明在南方分支從北方分支分離出來之後，婆羅門人和拜火教徒的祖先仍在一段時間內由共同語言的紐帶所聯繫。同樣的結論也可從哥德語 thusundi 與古普魯士語 tusimtons（受格）、立陶宛語 tukstantis、古斯拉夫語 tūisasta 之間的一致性推出；而希臘人和羅馬人則各自獨立地立足於其他所有人之外，似乎各自獨立地為千這一概念創造了自己的名稱。
那麼這個最早的時期，先於任何民族分化之前，就是我所謂的「神話制造時期」（mythopanic period），因為這些共同的雅利安詞彙中的每一個，在某種意義上都是一個神話。這些詞彙原本都是通名；它們表達了某個對象的眾多屬性中的一個，而這些屬性似乎是那個對象的特徵，這些屬性的篩選及其在語言中的表現，代表了一種無意識的詩性，而現代語言已經完全喪失了這種詩性。

語言被稱為「化石詩歌」。但正如藝術家在雕塑手中的黏土時，並不知道其中含有有機生命的遺跡，我們在稱呼父親時，亦未能感知我們實際上是在稱呼他為保護者；希臘人使用 δαήρ（dáēr，妹夫）一詞時，也不知道這個詞最初只適用於丈夫的弟弟們——他們留在家裡陪伴新娘，而他們的哥哥則在田野或森林裡勞作。梵文的 devar 原本意指玩伴——它述說著自己的故事——它是一個神話；但在希臘文中，它已經退化為一個單純的名字，或一個專門術語。然而，即使在希臘文中，人們也不被允許由 δαήρ 構成陰性形式，就如同我們即使在現今也不敢由「女兒」來構成陽性形式。

然而，語言很快便喪失了其詞源學的良知，因此在拉丁文中我們不僅發現了 vidua（無夫的；彭涅羅珀長期 -idua -iro suo caruit），而且還有 -iduus 這樣的詞形，若從詞源學上分析，其荒謬程度不亞於日耳曼語的 a v-idower。但必須坦承，古拉丁文 v-iduus（冥神歐爾庫斯〔Orcus〕之名，他在羅馬城外有一座廟宇）的存在，使得拉丁文 vidua 是否真的源自梵文 -idhava 一事顯得可疑，儘管兩者相似程度甚大；除非我們承認動詞 viduare 是從 vidua 衍生而來，而後來又據以形成了一個意義更為廣泛的新形容詞，因此 -iduus 對於羅馬人的耳朵而言，其含義無非就是 privatus（被剝奪的）而已。

但有人或許會問——雅利安語族共同保有這些古代名稱的事實，甚或發現所有這些名稱最初都具有表達力與詩意的力量，這一點究竟如何呢？¹ ¹ 與希臘文 δαΐδρι（源自根 δαί，意為「遊戲」）相同的，乃是拉丁文 *dīvir*。字母 *d* 與 *l* 經常互換，例如：立陶宛文 *dīva*（十）與希臘文 δέκα；*lacrumal* 與 Βακρύ；*dingua* 與 *lingua*；Ulysses 與 Ὀδυσσεύς；*odor* 與 *olfacio*、*presidium* 與 *praesidium*（Curtius, i. 275）。² ² Härtung, *Die Religion der Römer*, ii. 90.
比較神話學能否解釋這個家族所有成員中神話學語言的現象？它如何使我們理解人類心靈的那個階段——那個產生了關於諸神與英雄、戈爾貢與奇美拉的非凡故事的階段——那些人類眼睛從未見過、心靈在健全狀態下也絕不可能想像的事物？
在回答這個問題之前，我們必須先進行一些更多的預備性觀察，涉及詞彙的形成。雖然這似乎單調乏味，但我們相信，在進行這些考察的過程中，神話學的迷霧將逐漸消散，使我們能夠在思想與語言曙光飄浮的雲層背後，發現神話學長久以來所隱蔽與偽裝的真實本質。
